Welcome back and welcome to part 2 of Sales Mastery on Leash, the No BS System for Closing Sales like Crazy. In this segment we're going to be going over the first four steps of the No BS Sales System, including how to create an irresistible offer. So let's get started with right this right away. We talked a lot about this in the last segment. So I want to move right to the offer and the construction of the offer because, truthfully, most people get this wrong and without a good offer it's really tough for the salesperson to actually make the sale. So Dan, what are some of the elements that need to be put into a good offer? You want to give a salesperson the best, as you say, the best possible proposition that they can put in front of somebody that you can afford. Ideally if you have professionals you may want to give them some flexible pieces and parts that they can add on, they can modify, etc. If you have sort of lower level sales people like in a retail store, you don't do that, you've got to give them a locked in place. So here's what it is. Some of the elements are, depending on what's being sold, risk reversal. So what is that? Well, by some measure of guarantee, free trial, apparent free trial, some version of being able to get out without being wounded if this turns out not to be as represented. So you really don't need it with a jacket because you can try it on, when you pick it up you can look at it in the mirror, you can decide. Automobile, different story, mattress, different story. It always feels different 10 days after you got it on or sleeping on it than it did laying on a store and everybody knows it. So you have to think about what level of risk reversal is needed for it to be irresistible to your point. And then you try and get as close to that as you possibly can. A ratio of cost to economic value if you are selling money at a discount one way or another or ratio to emotional value if you are not selling money at a discount. Can you just explain what selling money at a discount is? Because I don't think a lot of people will know what that is. Well, so money, it's really very simple. It allows the presentation of the offer to ultimately come down to if you knew they were real, you had no question about their validity and your bank had $100 bills on sale today for $85.00, how many would you want and what lengths would you go to to round up as many as $85.00 as possible to go exchange for the $100.00? That's money at a discount. So to some people, so net jets, fractional jet ownership sells that as money at a discount, A, based on the value of the time of the user because most people who are buying one fourth of an airplane in order to fly private, their income is very high, therefore their time is very valuable. So a case can be made if I can get you back home today so you get to sleep in your own bed, be refreshed and come to the office in the morning ready to play versus you getting home at 11 o'clock at night if the flight's on time, not having good food and being irritated and what that has value, right? They also sell by emotional logic, most net jets advertising shows the happy family, the younger wife, the kids, so it's the second family with the fairly affluent gentleman on the beach that he's able to be with them in South Carolina and be at a board meeting two hours later because he's a net jets customer. So the ratios between value and cost are part of the offer, the actual description of the stuff, you get this and you get this and you get this, often great premiums can help or drive a sale, sometimes they're tied to risk reversal which is even if you don't like X and return it for a refund you get to keep the bonus, the premium.
